title: Find kth smallest element using heap
description: Find the kth smallest element using a min/max heap

examples.explanation: Step 1: Input array is [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9], k=3. Step 2: Sort the array in ascending order. Compare and rearrange elements: Start with [3,1,4,1,5,9]. After sorting passes: [1,1,3,4,5,9]. Step 3: Access element at index k-1 = 2 (0-indexed). arr[2] = 3. Step 4: Return 3.
Step 1: Input array is [7,10,4,3,20,15], k=4. Step 2: Sort the array: [3,4,7,10,15,20]. Step 3: Access index k-1 = 3. arr[3] = 10. Step 4: Return 10.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Sort the entire array in ascending order and directly access the element at index k-1. This is straightforward but inefficient if only the kth smallest is needed.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) or O(n) depending on sorting algorithm

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find kth smallest element using brute force sorting
ladders.desc: Sort the entire array and directly access the kth smallest element at index k-1
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to finding the kth smallest element leverages the fundamental property that sorting arranges elements in ascending order. Once sorted, the element at position k-1 (0-indexed) is guaranteed to be the kth smallest. This works because sorting transforms the array into a perfectly ordered sequence where each position corresponds to a specific rank. The intuition is simple: if we want to know which element is the kth smallest, we first organize all elements from smallest to largest, then count to the kth position. This approach is straightforward but inefficient for large datasets when only one element is needed, as it processes all n elements through sorting. The algorithm involves: 1) Create a copy of the input array to avoid modifying the original (optional but good practice). 2) Sort the array in ascending order using any comparison-based sorting algorithm. 3) Return the element at index k-1. Time complexity is O(n log n) due to sorting, and space complexity is O(1) if sorting in-place or O(n) if using a copy. This connects to the broader problem by providing a baseline solution that's easy to understand but not optimal for single queries. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle k=0 or k>n, not using 0-indexing correctly, and modifying the original array unintentionally. The real pattern involves using built-in sort functions with proper boundary checks.

examples.trace: Step 1: Input array is [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9], k=3. Step 2: Sort the array in ascending order. Compare and rearrange elements: Start with [3,1,4,1,5,9]. After sorting passes: [1,1,3,4,5,9]. Step 3: Access element at index k-1 = 2 (0-indexed). arr[2] = 3. Step 4: Return 3.
Step 1: Input array is [7,10,4,3,20,15], k=4. Step 2: Sort the array: [3,4,7,10,15,20]. Step 3: Access index k-1 = 3. arr[3] = 10. Step 4: Return 10.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Sorting the entire array
ladders.desc: Implement a sorting algorithm to arrange elements in ascending order
ladders.explanation: The key sub-routine in the brute force approach is sorting the array, which transforms an unordered collection into a sequence where each element's position reflects its rank. This works because sorting algorithms compare and rearrange elements based on their values, ensuring that after completion, for any index i, all elements before i are smaller or equal, and all after are larger or equal. The intuition is that by establishing total order, we can directly locate the kth smallest through indexing. We'll use quicksort as an example for its average O(n log n) performance. The algorithm: 1) Choose a pivot element (e.g., last element). 2) Partition the array so elements smaller than pivot go left, larger go right. 3) Recursively sort left and right partitions. Pseudocode: function sort(arr, low, high): if low < high: pivot_index = partition(arr, low, high); sort(arr, low, pivot_index-1); sort(arr, pivot_index+1, high). Time complexity is O(n log n) on average, O(n²) worst-case; space is O(log n) for recursion stack. This feeds into L0 by providing the sorted array needed for direct access. Common mistakes: not handling base cases in recursion, poor pivot selection leading to degraded performance, and off-by-one errors in indices. The pattern involves recursive partitioning with careful index management.

examples.trace: Step 1: Input [3,1,4,1,5]. Step 2: Quicksort with pivot=5: partition: compare 3<5, 1<5, 4<5, 1<5 → array unchanged, pivot at index 4. Step 3: Recursively sort left [3,1,4,1]: pivot=1, partition: 3>1, 4>1, 1=1 → swap to get [1,1,4,3], pivot at index 1. Step 4: Sort left of pivot [1] (single element, sorted). Sort right [4,3]: pivot=3, partition: 4>3 → swap to [3,4], pivot at index 2. Step 5: Combine: [1,1,3,4,5].

Step 1: Input [7,2,9,1]. Step 2: Quicksort pivot=1: partition: 7>1, 2>1, 9>1 → swap pivot to front: [1,2,9,7], pivot at index 0. Step 3: Sort right [2,9,7]: pivot=7, partition: 2<7, 9>7 → swap to [2,7,9], pivot at index 1. Step 4: Sort left [2] (sorted). Sort right [9] (sorted). Step 5: Combine: [1,2,7,9].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Direct indexing after sorting
ladders.desc: Access the element at position k-1 in a sorted array to find the kth smallest
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the brute force approach is that once an array is sorted in ascending order, the element at index k-1 (0-indexed) is the kth smallest. This works because sorting establishes a bijection between indices and ranks: index 0 holds the 1st smallest, index 1 holds the 2nd smallest, and so on. The intuition is that ordering eliminates uncertainty about relative positions, allowing direct lookup. This is a simple but powerful insight: instead of comparing elements repeatedly to find the kth smallest, we precompute all order relationships via sorting, then use constant-time indexing. The algorithm: given a sorted array S and integer k, return S[k-1]. Time complexity is O(1) for the access itself, but depends on sorting; space is O(1). This feeds into L1 by being the final step after sorting. Common mistakes: using 1-indexing incorrectly (e.g., accessing S[k]), not validating that k is within 1 to n, and assuming the array is sorted when it might not be. The pattern involves a single array access with bounds checking.

examples.trace: Step 1: Sorted array is [1,3,5,7,9]. Step 2: k=3, so index = k-1 = 2. Step 3: Access element at index 2: arr[2] = 5. Step 4: Return 5.
Step 1: Sorted array is [2,4,6,8,10,12]. Step 2: k=5, index = 4. Step 3: Access arr[4] = 10. Step 4: Return 10.

ladders.title: Building Block — Array indexing and bounds checking
ladders.desc: Access elements in an array by index and ensure the index is valid
ladders.explanation: This building block involves retrieving an element from an array using its index, which is fundamental to the brute force approach where we access the kth element after sorting. It works because arrays provide constant-time access to elements based on their position. The intuition is that an array is a contiguous block of memory where each element has a unique index starting from 0, allowing direct addressing. The algorithm: given an array A and an index i, return A[i] if i is within 0 and length(A)-1 inclusive; otherwise, handle the error (e.g., return an error value or throw exception). Time complexity is O(1), space is O(1). This feeds into L2 by enabling the direct access to the kth smallest element. Common mistakes: accessing out-of-bounds indices leading to runtime errors, using 1-indexing instead of 0-indexing, and not validating input indices. The pattern involves a simple conditional check before access.

examples.trace: Step 1: Array is [10,20,30,40], length=4. Step 2: Check index bounds: 2 is between 0 and 3 inclusive, so valid. Step 3: Access element at index 2: arr[2] = 30. Step 4: Return 30.
Step 1: Array [5,15,25], length=3. Step 2: Check bounds: 0 is between 0 and 2, valid. Step 3: Access arr[0] = 5. Step 4: Return 5.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing two elements
ladders.desc: Determine the relative order of two elements using comparison operators
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves comparing two elements to decide which is smaller, larger, or equal, which is essential for sorting in the brute force approach. It works because comparison operators (e.g., <, >, ==) define an order relation between elements. The intuition is that by comparing pairs of elements, we can deduce their relative positions in a sorted sequence. The algorithm: given two elements a and b, return -1 if a < b, 0 if a == b, or 1 if a > b (or use boolean results). Time complexity is O(1), space is O(1). This feeds into L3 as sorting algorithms rely on repeated comparisons to rearrange elements. Common mistakes: comparing incompatible types, not handling edge cases like NaN in floats, and incorrect operator usage. The pattern involves simple conditional statements or built-in comparison functions.

examples.trace: Step 1: a=5, b=3. Step 2: Compare using >: 5 > 3 is true. Step 3: Return true or 1 to indicate a is greater.

examples.output: 0 (or False for a < b and a > b)
examples.trace: Step 1: a=7, b=7. Step 2: Compare using ==: 7 == 7 is true. Step 3: Return 0 or false for inequalities, true for equality.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding arrays and indices
ladders.desc: Learn what an array is and how to access elements using indices
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces arrays, which are fundamental data structures used in the brute force approach to store and access elements. An array is a collection of elements, each identified by an index starting from 0. It works by allocating contiguous memory locations, allowing direct access to any element in constant time using its index. The intuition is that think of an array like a row of boxes numbered 0, 1, 2, etc.; to get an item, you just look in the box with that number. The algorithm: declare an array (e.g., arr = [10, 20, 30]), then access an element by index (e.g., arr[0] gives 10). Time complexity for access is O(1), space depends on number of elements. This feeds into L4 as comparisons operate on elements retrieved from arrays. Common mistakes: confusing 0-indexing with 1-indexing, accessing indices beyond the array size, and not initializing arrays properly. The pattern involves creating arrays and using square brackets for indexing.

examples.trace: Step 1: Define array arr = [5, 10, 15]. Step 2: Indices are 0, 1, 2 corresponding to values 5, 10, 15. Step 3: Access index 1: arr[1] = 10. Step 4: Return 10.
Step 1: Define array arr = [2, 4, 6, 8]. Step 2: Indices: 0→2, 1→4, 2→6, 3→8. Step 3: Access index 0: arr[0] = 2. Step 4: Return 2.

approaches.name: Max Heap O(n log k)
approaches.summary: Use a max heap of size k to track the k smallest elements seen so far. Iterate through the array, adding elements and removing the largest when heap size exceeds k, resulting in the kth smallest at the heap root.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find kth Smallest Element Using Max Heap
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to find the kth smallest element in an array using a max heap of size k.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is to maintain a max heap that always contains the k smallest elements encountered so far. As we iterate through the array, we add each element to the heap. If the heap size exceeds k, we remove the largest element (the root of the max heap), ensuring the heap only holds the k smallest elements seen. After processing all elements, the root of the max heap will be the kth smallest element because it's the largest among the k smallest elements in the entire array. This works because a max heap efficiently keeps track of the maximum among the smallest elements, which is exactly what we need for the kth smallest. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Initialize an empty max heap. 2) Iterate through each element in the array. 3) Push the current element into the heap. 4) If heap size > k, pop the largest element (heap root). 5) After iteration, return the root of the heap as the kth smallest. Time complexity is O(n log k) since we perform heap operations (push/pop) for each of n elements, each taking O(log k) time. Space complexity is O(k) for storing the heap. Connection to L1: This full solution relies on the key subroutine of maintaining the heap size at k. Common mistakes include using a min heap instead of max heap, which would require different logic, or not handling edge cases like k > array length. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: import heapq (using negative values for max heap), heap = [], for num in arr: heapq.heappush(heap, -num), if len(heap) > k: heapq.heappop(heap), return -heap[0].

examples.trace: Initialize max heap = []. Process arr[0]=3: heap becomes [-3], size=1 ≤ k=2. arr[1]=2: push -2, heap = [-3, -2], size=2 ≤ k. arr[2]=1: push -1, heap = [-3, -2, -1], size=3 > k, pop largest (root -1), heap = [-2, -3]. arr[3]=5: push -5, heap = [-5, -3, -2], size=3 > k, pop largest (root -2), heap = [-3, -5]. arr[4]=6: push -6, heap = [-6, -5, -3], size=3 > k, pop largest (root -3), heap = [-5, -6]. arr[5]=4: push -4, heap = [-6, -5, -4], size=3 > k, pop largest (root -4), heap = [-5, -6]. Final heap root = -5, return 5? Wait, check: heap stores negatives, so root -5 means value 5, but k=2 should return 2. Error in trace: Actually, after arr[2]=1: heap = [-3, -2, -1], pop largest means remove -1 (value 1), heap becomes [-3, -2]. Continue correctly: arr[3]=5: push -5, heap = [-5, -2, -3], size=3 > k, pop largest (root -2, value 2), heap = [-5, -3]. arr[4]=6: push -6, heap = [-6, -3, -5], size=3 > k, pop largest (root -3, value 3), heap = [-6, -5]. arr[5]=4: push -4, heap = [-6, -5, -4], size=3 > k, pop largest (root -4, value 4), heap = [-6, -5]. Final heap root = -6, value 6, but k=2 should be 2. Let's redo carefully: Start heap = []. arr[0]=3: push -3, heap = [-3], size=1. arr[1]=2: push -2, heap = [-3, -2], size=2. arr[2]=1: push -1, heap = [-3, -2, -1], size=3 > k=2, pop largest: heappop removes -1 (since max heap via negatives, -1 is largest negative, corresponding to value 1), heap becomes [-3, -2]. arr[3]=5: push -5, heap = [-5, -2, -3], size=3 > k, pop largest: root is -2 (value 2), remove it, heap becomes [-5, -3]. arr[4]=6: push -6, heap = [-6, -3, -5], size=3 > k, pop largest: root is -3 (value 3), remove, heap becomes [-6, -5]. arr[5]=4: push -4, heap = [-6, -5, -4], size=3 > k, pop largest: root is -4 (value 4), remove, heap becomes [-6, -5]. Final heap = [-6, -5], root = -6 (value 6). But k=2, kth smallest should be 2. There's a logic flaw: we need kth smallest, so after full array, heap should contain the two smallest: 1 and 2, root max=2. Our heap has 5 and 6. This is because max heap tracks k smallest, but we popped smaller values early. Correct approach: we should only pop when size > k, but we need to ensure heap always has k smallest. Let's trace correctly: arr = [3,2,1,5,6,4], k=2. Heap after each step: 1) 3: heap=[3], size=1. 2) 2: push 2, heap=[3,2] as max heap (largest at root: 3), size=2. 3) 1: push 1, heap=[3,2,1], size=3>k, pop largest (3), heap=[2,1]. 4) 5: push 5, heap=[5,1,2], size=3>k, pop largest (5), heap=[2,1]. 5) 6: push 6, heap=[6,1,2], size=3>k, pop largest (6), heap=[2,1]. 6) 4: push 4, heap=[4,1,2], size=3>k, pop largest (4), heap=[2,1]. Final heap root=2 (largest among {1,2}), so kth smallest=2. Using negatives: heap stores negatives, so comparisons flip. Let's do with negatives: 1) 3: push -3, heap=[-3]. 2) 2: push -2, heap=[-3,-2] (root -3, value 3). 3) 1: push -1, heap=[-3,-2,-1], size>2, pop largest negative: heappop removes -1 (value 1), but wait, in max heap via negatives, -1 is largest negative, so it corresponds to smallest value 1. We want to remove largest value, which is 3 (negative -3). So we need to pop the root, which for max heap (using negatives) is the most negative, i.e., smallest value. To simulate max heap, we push negative values, so heap root is most negative (smallest original value). To get max original value, we need to pop the least negative. But heapq is min-heap, so with negatives, the heap root is the most negative (smallest original). To remove largest original, we need to pop the element with largest negative? Actually, if we store negatives, then original value x becomes -x in heap. The heap is min-heap on these negatives. The smallest negative corresponds to largest original. Example: values 3,2,1 become -3,-2,-1. Min-heap root is -3 (smallest negative), which is largest original (3). So to remove largest original, we pop root. Yes! So in step 3: heap=[-3,-2,-1], pop root: heappop gives -3 (value 3), heap becomes [-2,-1]. Then continue: 4) 5: push -5, heap=[-5,-1,-2], pop root: -5 (value 5), heap=[-2,-1]. 5) 6: push -6, heap=[-6,-1,-2], pop root: -6 (value 6), heap=[-2,-1]. 6) 4: push -4, heap=[-4,-1,-2], pop root: -4 (value 4), heap=[-2,-1]. Final heap root = -2 (value 2), return 2. Perfect.

Initialize max heap = []. k=3. Process arr[0]=7: push -7, heap=[-7], size=1. arr[1]=10: push -10, heap=[-10,-7], size=2. arr[2]=4: push -4, heap=[-10,-7,-4], size=3. arr[3]=3: push -3, heap=[-10,-7,-4,-3], size=4 > k=3, pop root (largest original): heappop removes -10 (value 10), heap becomes [-7,-4,-3]. arr[4]=20: push -20, heap=[-20,-4,-3,-7], size=4 > k, pop root: remove -20 (value 20), heap becomes [-7,-4,-3]. arr[5]=15: push -15, heap=[-15,-4,-3,-7], size=4 > k, pop root: remove -15 (value 15), heap becomes [-7,-4,-3]. Final heap root = -7 (value 7), so kth smallest = 7. Verify: sorted array = [3,4,7,10,15,20], 3rd smallest is 7.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Maintain Max Heap of Size k
ladders.desc: Implement the core function that processes an array element and maintains a max heap with exactly k elements.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the full solution. The intuition is to dynamically update a max heap so it always contains the k smallest elements processed so far. For each new element from the array, we add it to the heap. If after adding, the heap size exceeds k, we remove the largest element (the root of the max heap). This ensures the heap never grows beyond k and always holds the k smallest elements encountered. This works because by removing the largest whenever we exceed k, we discard elements that cannot be among the k smallest in the final result. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Accept current heap, new element, and k as inputs. 2) Push the new element onto the max heap (using negative values for max heap simulation). 3) If heap size > k, pop the root element (which is the largest in the heap). 4) Return the updated heap. Time complexity per call is O(log k) for heap operations. Space complexity is O(k) for the heap storage. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called repeatedly in the full problem's loop over the array. Common mistakes include not simulating max heap correctly with negatives, or popping when size equals k instead of greater than k. Real code pattern: def maintain_heap(heap, num, k): heapq.heappush(heap, -num); if len(heap) > k: heapq.heappop(heap); return heap.

examples.trace: Start with empty heap. Push -5 onto heap (simulating max heap for value 5). Heap becomes [-5]. Size=1 ≤ k=2, so no pop. Return heap [-5].

Initial heap represents values 3 and 1 (since negatives). Push -2 for new value 2: heap becomes [-3, -1, -2]. Size=3 > k=2, so pop root. Root is most negative: -3 (value 3), remove it. Heap becomes [-2, -1] after heapify. Return heap with values 2 and 1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Tracking k Smallest Elements with Max Heap
ladders.desc: Understand why a max heap of size k can efficiently find the kth smallest element.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that by maintaining a max heap limited to k elements, we can keep track of the k smallest values from the processed portion of the array. The max heap property ensures the largest element among these k is at the root. When we add a new element, if it's smaller than the current root (the largest of the k smallest), it should replace that root to maintain the k smallest set. This is achieved by always adding the new element and then removing the root if heap size exceeds k. After processing all elements, the root of the heap is the kth smallest because it's the largest among the k smallest elements in the entire array. The intuition: imagine you have a list of the k smallest so far; when you see a new element, if it's smaller than the maximum in that list, it belongs in the list, and you kick out the old maximum. This dynamic updating ensures optimal tracking. Step-by-step: 1) Start with empty heap. 2) For each element x: add x to heap; if heap size > k, remove max. 3) Final heap root is kth smallest. Time O(n log k), space O(k). Connection to L1: This logic is implemented by the subroutine that maintains the heap. Common mistakes: confusing min heap and max heap roles. Real code pattern: conceptual understanding without code.

examples.input: Array so far: [5, 2, 8], k = 2, current heap = [5,2] (max heap, root=5)
examples.trace: Current heap has values 5 and 2, with 5 as largest (root). New element 8 is larger than root 5, so if added, heap becomes [8,2,5], size=3>k=2, remove max (8), heap returns to [5,2]. Thus, 8 is ignored as it can't be among 2 smallest.

examples.input: Array so far: [10, 7, 3], k = 2, current heap = [10,7]
examples.trace: Heap has 10 and 7, root=10. New element 3 is smaller than root 10, so add 3: heap becomes [10,7,3], size=3>k, remove max (10), heap becomes [7,3]. Now heap contains the two smallest: 7 and 3.

testCases.input: Current heap values: [9, 5], new element: 6, k=2
Current heap values: [4, 1], new element: 3, k=2
Current heap values: [8, 3, 2], new element: 7, k=3
Current heap values: [10], new element: 10, k=1
Current heap values: [6, 4], new element: 5, k=2
Current heap values: [100, 50], new element: 75, k=2
Current heap values: [2, 1], new element: 0, k=2
Current heap values: [5, 3, 1], new element: 4, k=3
Current heap values: [7], new element: 2, k=1
Current heap values: [9, 8, 7], new element: 6, k=3

ladders.title: Building Block — Heap Push and Pop Operations
ladders.desc: Learn how to add elements to and remove the root from a heap, specifically simulating a max heap using a min heap.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept covers the fundamental heap operations needed for the algorithm. A heap is a complete binary tree that satisfies the heap property: for a max heap, each node is greater than or equal to its children; for a min heap, each node is less than or equal to its children. In many programming languages, only min heaps are directly supported, so we simulate a max heap by storing negative values. The intuition: by negating values, the order reverses, so the min heap of negatives behaves like a max heap for the original values. For example, original values 5, 3 become -5, -3 in a min heap; -5 is smaller than -3, so -5 is at the root, corresponding to the largest original value 5. Step-by-step operations: Push: add element to the end of the heap array, then bubble up to maintain heap property. Pop: remove the root (first element), replace with last element, then bubble down. Time complexity for both is O(log n) where n is heap size. Space O(1) per operation. Connection to L2: These operations are used to maintain the heap of size k. Common mistakes: forgetting to negate values when simulating max heap, or mishandling heap indices. Real code pattern using heapq: for max heap, push -value, pop gives -value to retrieve original.

examples.trace: Start heap [3,5,8] (min heap, root=3). Add 1 at end: [3,5,8,1]. Bubble up: compare 1 with parent 8 at index 1, 1<8, swap: [3,1,8,5]. Compare 1 with parent 3 at index 0, 1<3, swap: [1,3,8,5]. Final heap [1,3,8,5].

examples.input: Max heap simulation: initial negatives [-5, -3, -1] (values 5,3,1). Pop root.
examples.trace: Heap as negatives: [-5,-3,-1]. Pop root: remove -5 (value 5), replace with last element -1: heap becomes [-1,-3]. Bubble down: compare -1 with children -3, -1 is larger than -3? Wait, in min heap of negatives, -1 is larger than -3? Actually, -1 > -3 numerically, but for min heap, we compare values: -1 is not less than -3, so swap with smaller child. Children are at indices 1 and 2: -3 and (none for index 2 since only two elements). Compare -1 and -3: -3 is smaller, so swap: heap becomes [-3,-1]. Final heap [-3,-1], corresponding to values 3 and 1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare and Swap Elements in a Heap
ladders.desc: Understand how to maintain heap property by comparing parent and child nodes and swapping if necessary.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is key to heap integrity. The heap property depends on comparisons between parent and child nodes. For a min heap, each parent must be less than or equal to its children; for a max heap, each parent must be greater than or equal to its children. When adding or removing elements, we may violate this property, so we perform compare-and-swap operations to restore it. The intuition: by repeatedly comparing a node with its parent (during bubble up) or with its children (during bubble down), and swapping if the order is wrong, we ensure the heap property holds throughout the tree. Step-by-step: For bubble up after push: start at the new element, compare with parent; if out of order, swap and repeat until root or order correct. For bubble down after pop: start at root, compare with children; if out of order, swap with the smaller child (for min heap) or larger child (for max heap) and repeat until leaf or order correct. Time O(log n) per operation due to tree height. Space O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used within push and pop. Common mistakes: incorrect child index calculations or comparison directions. Real code pattern: while index > 0 and heap[index] < heap[parent]: swap.

examples.input: Min heap array: [3, 5, 8, 6]. Bubble up element at index 3 (value 6).
examples.trace: Element at index 3 is 6. Parent index = (3-1)//2 = 1, value 5. Compare 6 and 5: 6 >= 5, so no swap. Heap remains [3,5,8,6].

examples.input: Max heap simulation array: [-5, -3, -1, -4] (values 5,3,1,4). Bubble down root after pop simulation.
examples.trace: Assume root removed and replaced with last element -4: array [-4,-3,-1]. Bubble down root at index 0, value -4. Children at indices 1 and 2: -3 and -1. For max heap simulation (min heap on negatives), we swap with smaller child? Actually, to maintain min heap on negatives, we want parent <= children. Compare -4 with -3: -4 <= -3, good. Compare -4 with -1: -4 <= -1, good. So no swap, heap [-4,-3,-1].

testCases.input: Bubble up in min heap [2, 4, 3, 1] at index 3
Bubble down in min heap [5, 3, 4] from root
Bubble up in max heap sim [-2, -5, -6] at index 2
Bubble down in max heap sim [-1, -3, -2] from root
Bubble up in min heap [10] at index 0
Bubble down in min heap [7, 8] from root
Bubble up in max heap sim [-9, -10] at index 1
Bubble down in max heap sim [-4] from root
Bubble up in min heap [6, 7, 8, 9, 5] at index 4
Bubble down in min heap [4, 2, 3] from root

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Heap?
ladders.desc: Learn the basic definition and properties of a heap data structure.
ladders.explanation: A heap is a specialized tree-based data structure that satisfies the heap property. It is commonly implemented as a binary heap, which is a complete binary tree. The heap property defines the ordering: in a max heap, for any given node, its value is greater than or equal to the values of its children; in a min heap, its value is less than or equal to the values of its children. This property ensures that the root node always contains the maximum (in max heap) or minimum (in min heap) element, making heaps efficient for priority queue operations. Heaps are typically stored in arrays, where for a node at index i, its left child is at index 2i+1, right child at 2i+2, and parent at floor((i-1)/2). The intuition behind heaps is to provide fast access to the extreme value (min or max) while allowing efficient insertions and deletions. They are used in algorithms like heap sort and priority scheduling. Step-by-step: to create a heap, you can build it from an array in O(n) time. Basic operations include insert (push) and extract-min/max (pop), both O(log n). Connection to L4: Understanding heap structure is prerequisite for performing compare-and-swap operations. Common mistakes: confusing heap with binary search tree. Real code pattern: heap = [] in Python, using list to represent tree.

examples.input: Array representation: [10, 5, 3, 2, 4]. Is this a max heap?
examples.trace: Check heap property: root 10, left child 5 (10>=5, ok), right child 3 (10>=3, ok). For node 5: left child 2 (5>=2, ok), right child 4 (5>=4, ok). So it satisfies max heap property? Wait, array [10,5,3,2,4] as complete tree: level 0: 10; level 1: 5 and 3; level 2: 2 and 4 as children of 5. Yes, 10>=5 and 10>=3; 5>=2 and 5>=4. So it is a max heap. Output should be Yes.

examples.input: Min heap with values [1, 3, 2, 6, 4]. Find the parent of element 4.
examples.trace: Array indices: 0:1, 1:3, 2:2, 3:6, 4:4. Element 4 is at index 4. Parent index = (4-1)//2 = 1. Value at index 1 is 3. So parent is 3.

testCases.input: In heap [7, 4, 6, 1, 3], what is left child of 4?
In heap [9, 8, 7], what is parent of 7?
In heap [2, 5, 3], what is right child of 2?
In heap [6, 4, 5, 2, 3], what is parent of 3?
In heap [1, 2, 3, 4], what is left child of 2?

approaches.name: Min Heap O(n + k log n)
approaches.summary: Build a min heap from all elements in O(n) time, then extract the minimum k times to get the kth smallest. This is useful when multiple queries are needed or k is small.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find kth smallest element using min heap O(n + k log n)
ladders.desc: Complete implementation: build min heap from array, extract min k times
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the kth smallest element problem by first transforming the input array into a min heap in O(n) time using heapify. A min heap is a complete binary tree where each node's value is less than or equal to its children's values, guaranteeing the smallest element is at the root. After heap construction, we perform k extract-min operations. Each extract-min removes the root (smallest element), replaces it with the last heap element, then heapifies down to restore the heap property. The kth extracted element is our answer. The time complexity is O(n) for heap construction plus O(k log n) for k extractions, making it efficient when k is small relative to n. Space complexity is O(n) for the heap. This approach is particularly useful when multiple kth smallest queries are needed on the same dataset, as the heap can be reused. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle edge cases like k > n, using 1-based vs 0-based indexing incorrectly, or not properly maintaining heap size during extractions. The key insight is that repeated extraction from a min heap naturally yields elements in sorted order without fully sorting the array.

examples.trace:
1. Build min heap from [7,10,4,3,20,15]: heapify produces [3,7,4,10,20,15]
2. Extract min 1st time: remove 3, replace with 15, heapify down → heap becomes [4,7,15,10,20]
3. Extract min 2nd time: remove 4, replace with 20, heapify down → heap becomes [7,10,15,20]
4. Extract min 3rd time: remove 7 → this is the 3rd smallest element

1. Build min heap from [1,2,3,4,5]: already a min heap [1,2,3,4,5]
2. Extract min 1st: remove 1 → heap becomes [2,3,4,5]
3. Extract min 2nd: remove 2 → heap becomes [3,4,5]
4. Extract min 3rd: remove 3 → heap becomes [4,5]
5. Extract min 4th: remove 4 → heap becomes [5]
6. Extract min 5th: remove 5 → this is the 5th smallest element

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Extract-min operation from min heap
ladders.desc: Remove and return smallest element while maintaining heap property
ladders.explanation: The extract-min operation is the core of the kth smallest algorithm. It removes the root element (guaranteed to be the smallest in a min heap), replaces it with the last element in the heap, reduces heap size by 1, then performs heapify-down to restore the min heap property. The intuition is that by repeatedly extracting the minimum, we get elements in ascending order. The algorithm: 1) Store root value to return later. 2) Replace root with last element. 3) Decrease heap size. 4) Heapify down from root: compare with children, swap with smaller child if needed, repeat until heap property is restored. Time complexity is O(log n) since heapify-down traverses at most the height of the tree. Space is O(1). This connects to L0 as we perform this operation k times to get the kth smallest. Common mistakes include not checking if heap is empty before extraction, forgetting to update heap size, or incorrect heapify-down logic. The key insight is that removing the root disrupts the heap, but replacing with the last element and heapifying down efficiently restores order.

examples.trace:
1. Store root value 3
2. Replace root with last element 15 → [15,7,4,10,20]
3. Decrease size to 5
4. Heapify down from root (index 0):
   - Compare 15 with children 7 and 4
   - 4 is smaller than 7, swap 15 with 4 → [4,7,15,10,20]
   - Continue from index 2: compare 15 with children (none) → done

1. Store root value 1
2. Replace root with last element 3 → [3,2]
3. Decrease size to 2
4. Heapify down from root:
   - Compare 3 with child 2
   - 2 < 3, swap → [2,3]
   - Continue from index 1: no children → done

ladders.title: Core Logic — Heapify-down (sift-down) operation
ladders.desc: Restore min heap property by moving an element down to its correct position
ladders.explanation: Heapify-down is the algorithm that maintains the min heap property when an element might be larger than its children. Starting from a given index, we compare the element with its children. If it's larger than either child, we swap it with the smaller child (to maintain the min heap property where parent ≤ children). We then continue this process recursively from the new position until the element is in the correct place or reaches a leaf. The intuition is that violations of the heap property can be fixed by "sifting" the problematic element down the tree. In array representation, for index i, children are at 2i+1 and 2i+2. The algorithm: while current node has children: 1) Find smallest among current node and its children. 2) If current node is smallest, stop. 3) Otherwise, swap with smallest child. 4) Move to child's position and repeat. Time complexity is O(log n) as we traverse at most tree height. Space is O(1). This connects to L1 as heapify-down is used after extracting the root. Common mistakes include incorrect child index calculation, not handling the case where only one child exists, or infinite loops when stopping condition is wrong. The key insight is that swapping with the smaller child preserves the heap property locally while moving the problem downward.

examples.trace:
1. Index 0 value 15, children at 1 (7) and 2 (4)
2. Smallest among {15,7,4} is 4 at index 2
3. Swap 15 with 4 → [4,7,15,10,20]
4. Move to index 2, value 15, children at 5 and 6 (none, beyond array)
5. No children → stop

1. Index 0 value 10, children at 1 (5) and 2 (8)
2. Smallest among {10,5,8} is 5 at index 1
3. Swap 10 with 5 → [5,10,8,15,20]
4. Move to index 1, value 10, children at 3 (15) and 4 (20)
5. Smallest among {10,15,20} is 10 at current index
6. Current is smallest → stop

ladders.title: Building Block — Min heap construction from array (heapify)
ladders.desc: Transform an arbitrary array into a valid min heap in O(n) time
ladders.explanation: Heap construction is the process of converting an unsorted array into a min heap. The efficient O(n) algorithm works by starting from the last non-leaf node and applying heapify-down to each node moving upward. The intuition is that we can build the heap bottom-up: leaf nodes are already valid heaps (they have no children), so we start from the parent of the last leaf and work backward to the root. For an array of size n, the last non-leaf node is at index floor(n/2)-1. The algorithm: 1) Find last non-leaf index. 2) For i from last non-leaf down to 0: call heapify-down on index i. This ensures that by the time we reach the root, the entire tree satisfies the min heap property. Time complexity is O(n) due to a mathematical analysis showing most heapify operations work on small subtrees. Space is O(1) in-place. This connects to L2 as heapify-down is the subroutine used. Common mistakes include starting from the wrong index, iterating in the wrong direction, or misunderstanding why this is O(n). The key insight is that we only need to heapify non-leaf nodes, and the work decreases exponentially as we move up the tree.

examples.trace:
1. n=6, last non-leaf index = floor(6/2)-1 = 2
2. Start from index 2 (value 4): heapify-down on [7,10,4,3,20,15]
   - 4 has no children (indices 5,6) → no change
3. Index 1 (value 10): heapify-down
   - Compare 10 with children 3 and 20
   - Smaller child is 3, swap → [7,3,4,10,20,15]
   - Move to index 3 (value 10), no children → done
4. Index 0 (value 7): heapify-down
   - Compare 7 with children 3 and 4
   - Smaller child is 3, swap → [3,7,4,10,20,15]
   - Move to index 1 (value 7), compare with children 10 and 20
   - 7 is smaller than both → done
5. Final heap: [3,7,4,10,20,15]

1. n=5, last non-leaf index = floor(5/2)-1 = 1
2. Index 1 (value 4): heapify-down
   - Compare 4 with children 2 and 1
   - Smaller child is 1, swap → [5,1,3,2,4]
   - Move to index 4 (value 4), no children → done
3. Index 0 (value 5): heapify-down
   - Compare 5 with children 1 and 3
   - Smaller child is 1, swap → [1,5,3,2,4]
   - Move to index 1 (value 5), compare with children 2 and 4
   - Smaller child is 2, swap → [1,2,3,5,4]
   - Move to index 3 (value 5), no children → done
4. Final heap: [1,2,3,5,4]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare and swap elements in array
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of comparing two elements and swapping if needed
ladders.explanation: This is the atomic operation used throughout heap algorithms. Comparing two elements determines their relative order, while swapping exchanges their positions in the array. In heap operations, we frequently compare a parent with its children to check if the min heap property (parent ≤ children) is violated, and swap if needed to correct it. The intuition is that by repeatedly comparing and swapping, we can move elements to their correct positions in the heap. The algorithm for swapping two elements at indices i and j: 1) Store arr[i] in temporary variable. 2) Set arr[i] = arr[j]. 3) Set arr[j] = temporary. Comparison is simply using relational operators (<, >, ≤). Time complexity is O(1) for both operations. Space is O(1). This connects to L3 as heapify uses compare-and-swap repeatedly. Common mistakes include forgetting the temporary variable when swapping (which loses data), off-by-one errors in indices, or incorrect comparison logic. The key insight is that swapping is how we physically rearrange elements, while comparison tells us when swapping is needed.

examples.input: Array = [5,10], compare indices 0 and 1, swap if arr[0] > arr[1]
examples.trace:
1. Compare arr[0]=5 with arr[1]=10
2. 5 ≤ 10, so condition arr[0] > arr[1] is false
3. No swap performed
4. Array remains [5,10]

examples.input: Array = [10,5], compare indices 0 and 1, swap if arr[0] > arr[1]
examples.trace:
1. Compare arr[0]=10 with arr[1]=5
2. 10 > 5, so condition is true
3. Perform swap:
   - temp = arr[0] = 10
   - arr[0] = arr[1] = 5
   - arr[1] = temp = 10
4. Array becomes [5,10]

testCases.input: arr = [3,7], i=0, j=1, swap if arr[i] > arr[j]
arr = [8,2], i=0, j=1, swap if arr[i] > arr[j]
arr = [5,5], i=0, j=1, swap if arr[i] > arr[j]
arr = [10,1,5], i=0, j=2, swap if arr[i] > arr[j]
arr = [4,3,2], i=1, j=2, swap if arr[i] > arr[j]
arr = [6,5,4,3], i=0, j=3, swap if arr[i] > arr[j]
arr = [9,8,7], i=0, j=1, swap if arr[i] > arr[j]
arr = [15,10,20], i=1, j=2, swap if arr[i] > arr[j]
arr = [0,-5,5], i=0, j=1, swap if arr[i] > arr[j]
arr = [100,50,75], i=1, j=2, swap if arr[i] > arr[j]

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array representation of binary tree
ladders.desc: How to store a complete binary tree in an array using index relationships
ladders.explanation: A heap is a complete binary tree, which can be efficiently stored in an array without explicit node objects or pointers. In this representation, the root is at index 0. For any node at index i: its left child is at index 2i+1, right child at 2i+2, and parent at floor((i-1)/2). The tree is "complete" meaning all levels are fully filled except possibly the last, which is filled from left to right. This compact representation saves memory and allows efficient access via index arithmetic. The intuition is that we can navigate the tree using simple arithmetic on indices rather than following pointers. This is fundamental because heap algorithms rely on these index relationships to find parents and children quickly. For example, to heapify down from index i, we need to access children at 2i+1 and 2i+2. The array must be large enough to hold all elements, and we maintain a "heap size" variable that may be less than array length. Time complexity for accessing parent/child is O(1). Space is O(n). This connects to L4 as compare-and-swap operations use these indices. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in the formulas, confusing 0-based vs 1-based indexing, or not understanding what "complete binary tree" means. The key insight is that the array indices naturally correspond to a level-order traversal of the tree.

examples.output: Tree structure: root 3, left child 7, right child 4, etc.
examples.trace:
1. Index 0: value 3 (root)
2. Left child of index 0: 2*0+1 = index 1 → value 7
3. Right child of index 0: 2*0+2 = index 2 → value 4
4. Left child of index 1: 2*1+1 = index 3 → value 10
5. Right child of index 1: 2*1+2 = index 4 → value 20
6. Left child of index 2: 2*2+1 = index 5 → value 15
7. Parent of index 1: floor((1-1)/2) = 0 → value 3
8. Parent of index 4: floor((4-1)/2) = 1 → value 7

examples.output: Tree structure: root 1, left child 2, right child 3
examples.trace:
1. Index 0: value 1 (root)
2. Left child of 0: 2*0+1 = index 1 → value 2
3. Right child of 0: 2*0+2 = index 2 → value 3
4. Parent of index 2: floor((2-1)/2) = 0 → value 1
5. Index 1 has no children since 2*1+1=3 is beyond array length 3

testCases.input: Array = [5,10,15], find left child of index 0
Array = [5,10,15], find right child of index 0
Array = [1,2,3,4,5,6], find left child of index 2
Array = [8,4,12,2,6,10,14], find right child of index 1
Array = [100,50,75,25,30], find left child of index 1
Array = [7,14,21,28,35], find right child of index 0
Array = [2,4,6,8,10,12,14,16], find left child of index 3

approaches.name: QuickSelect Average O(n)
approaches.summary: Adapt the quicksort algorithm to recursively partition the array around a pivot, narrowing down to the kth smallest element without fully sorting. This offers average linear time complexity.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) or O(log n) for recursion

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find kth Smallest Element Using QuickSelect
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of QuickSelect algorithm to find kth smallest element in an unsorted array.
ladders.explanation: QuickSelect is a selection algorithm that finds the kth smallest element in an unsorted array by adapting the quicksort partitioning mechanism. The intuition is that after partitioning the array around a pivot, we can determine which partition contains the kth smallest element and recursively search only that partition, avoiding the need to fully sort the array. This gives us average O(n) time complexity, though worst-case is O(n²) if we consistently choose bad pivots. The algorithm works by: 1) Choosing a pivot element (commonly the last element), 2) Partitioning the array so elements smaller than pivot are on left, larger on right, 3) Determining pivot's position, 4) If pivot position equals k-1, we've found our element, 5) Otherwise recursively search left or right partition based on comparison. Space complexity is O(log n) for recursion stack in average case, O(n) worst-case. Connection to parent: This is the complete solution. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle 0-based vs 1-based indexing for k, not implementing proper base cases, choosing poor pivots leading to worst-case performance. Real code pattern: Use Lomuto partition scheme with last element as pivot, recursively narrow search space.

examples.trace: Initial: [7,10,4,3,20,15], k=3 (0-indexed: k-1=2). Partition 1: pivot=15, i=-1. j=0:7<15, swap arr[0] with arr[0], i=0. j=1:10<15, swap arr[1] with arr[1], i=1. j=2:4<15, swap arr[2] with arr[2], i=2. j=3:3<15, swap arr[3] with arr[3], i=3. j=4:20>15, no swap. j=5: pivot swap arr[5] with arr[4], pivot index=4. pivot_pos=4 > 2, search left [7,10,4,3]. Partition 2: pivot=3, i=-1. j=0:7>3, no swap. j=1:10>3, no swap. j=2:4>3, no swap. j=3: pivot swap arr[3] with arr[3], pivot index=3. pivot_pos=3 > 2, search left [7,10,4]. Partition 3: pivot=4, i=-1. j=0:7>4, no swap. j=1:10>4, no swap. j=2: pivot swap arr[2] with arr[2], pivot index=2. pivot_pos=2 == 2, return arr[2]=7.

Initial: [12,3,5,7,4,19,26], k=5 (0-indexed: 4). Partition 1: pivot=26, i=-1. j=0:12<26, swap arr[0] with arr[0], i=0. j=1:3<26, swap arr[1] with arr[1], i=1. j=2:5<26, swap arr[2] with arr[2], i=2. j=3:7<26, swap arr[3] with arr[3], i=3. j=4:4<26, swap arr[4] with arr[4], i=4. j=5:19<26, swap arr[5] with arr[5], i=5. j=6: pivot swap arr[6] with arr[6], pivot index=6. pivot_pos=6 > 4, search left [12,3,5,7,4,19]. Partition 2: pivot=19, i=-1. j=0:12<19, swap arr[0] with arr[0], i=0. j=1:3<19, swap arr[1] with arr[1], i=1. j=2:5<19, swap arr[2] with arr[2], i=2. j=3:7<19, swap arr[3] with arr[3], i=3. j=4:4<19, swap arr[4] with arr[4], i=4. j=5: pivot swap arr[5] with arr[5], pivot index=5. pivot_pos=5 > 4, search left [12,3,5,7,4]. Partition 3: pivot=4, i=-1. j=0:12>4, no swap. j=1:3<4, swap arr[1] with arr[1], i=1. j=2:5>4, no swap. j=3:7>4, no swap. j=4: pivot swap arr[4] with arr[2], pivot index=2. pivot_pos=2 < 4, search right [7,4] (note: after swap arr=[3,4,12,7,5]). Adjust k: k=4-2-1=1 (search for 1st smallest in right). Partition 4: pivot=5, i=-1. j=0:7>5, no swap. j=1: pivot swap arr[1] with arr[1], pivot index=1. pivot_pos=1 > 0, search left [7]. Base case: left array size 1, return 7. Backtrack: pivot_pos=2, right result=7, but we need element at original index 4, which is 12.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Partition Array Around Pivot
ladders.desc: Implement the partition function that rearranges array so elements smaller than pivot are on left, larger on right.
ladders.explanation: The partition function is the core operation in QuickSelect that enables the divide-and-conquer approach. The intuition is to choose a pivot element and rearrange the array such that all elements less than the pivot come before it, and all elements greater than or equal come after it. This creates a partially sorted state where the pivot ends up in its correct sorted position. Using Lomuto partition scheme: 1) Choose pivot (typically last element), 2) Initialize index i to track position where smaller elements should go, 3) Iterate through array with index j, 4) If current element < pivot, increment i and swap arr[i] with arr[j], 5) After loop, swap pivot with arr[i+1] to place pivot in correct position, 6) Return pivot index. Time complexity: O(n) for single partition. Space: O(1). Connection to level above: This partition enables QuickSelect to narrow search space. Common mistakes: Forgetting final pivot swap, incorrect boundary conditions, not handling equal elements properly. Real code pattern: Use two-pointer technique with last element as pivot.

examples.trace: Initial: [5,1,8,3,2,7,6,4], pivot=4, i=-1. j=0:5>4, no swap. j=1:1<4, i=0, swap arr[0] with arr[1] → [1,5,8,3,2,7,6,4]. j=2:8>4, no swap. j=3:3<4, i=1, swap arr[1] with arr[3] → [1,3,8,5,2,7,6,4]. j=4:2<4, i=2, swap arr[2] with arr[4] → [1,3,2,5,8,7,6,4]. j=5:7>4, no swap. j=6:6>4, no swap. Loop end: swap pivot arr[7] with arr[i+1=3] → [1,3,2,4,8,7,6,5]. Return pivot index=3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Search Space Reduction
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive logic that uses pivot position to narrow search to relevant partition.
ladders.explanation: The core insight of QuickSelect is that after partitioning, we can determine which side of the pivot contains the kth smallest element and recursively search only that partition. The intuition is based on the property that after partitioning, the pivot is in its final sorted position. If pivot position equals k-1 (0-indexed), we've found our answer. If pivot position > k-1, the kth smallest must be in the left partition. If pivot position < k-1, it must be in the right partition, but we need to adjust k since we're discarding left partition elements. This reduces problem size by approximately half on average, leading to O(n) average time. Steps: 1) Call partition to get pivot index, 2) Compare pivot index with k-1, 3) If equal, return arr[pivot_index], 4) If pivot_index > k-1, recursively search left partition with same k, 5) If pivot_index < k-1, recursively search right partition with k = k - pivot_index - 1. Time: O(n) average, O(n²) worst. Space: O(log n) recursion. Connection: This logic uses partition results. Common mistakes: Incorrect k adjustment for right search, not handling base cases. Real code pattern: Recursive function with pivot comparison.

examples.trace: Partition: pivot=26, arr becomes [10,4,5,8,6,11,26], pivot_index=6. pivot_index=6 > k-1=2, search left partition [10,4,5,8,6,11] (left=0, right=5). Partition: pivot=11, arr becomes [10,4,5,8,6,11], pivot_index=5. pivot_index=5 > 2, search left [10,4,5,8,6] (left=0, right=4). Partition: pivot=6, arr becomes [4,5,6,8,10], pivot_index=2. pivot_index=2 == 2, return arr[2]=6.
Partition: pivot=15, arr becomes [7,10,4,3,15,20], pivot_index=4. pivot_index=4 > k-1=4? Wait k=5 so k-1=4, pivot_index=4 == 4, return arr[4]=15.

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Element Swapping
ladders.desc: Implement in-place swapping of two elements in an array.
ladders.explanation: Swapping two elements is a fundamental operation used repeatedly in partition and other array algorithms. The intuition is simple: we need to exchange the values at two positions in the array. This is done using a temporary variable to hold one value while overwriting it. Steps: 1) Store value at index i in temp, 2) Assign value at index j to index i, 3) Assign temp to index j. This ensures both values are correctly exchanged without loss. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection: This operation is used in partition function to move elements. Common mistakes: Forgetting temp variable (direct assignment loses data), incorrect indices. Real code pattern: temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[j]; arr[j] = temp.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare Array Elements
ladders.desc: Compare two elements in an array to determine their relative order.
ladders.explanation: Comparing elements is the most basic operation in sorting and selection algorithms. The intuition is to determine whether one element is less than, greater than, or equal to another element. This comparison drives decisions in algorithms like partition. Steps: Access arr[i] and arr[j], use comparison operator (<, >, ==). Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection: Used in partition to decide whether to swap elements. Common mistakes: Comparing indices instead of values, off-by-one errors. Real code pattern: if arr[j] < pivot: ...

examples.trace: arr[0] = 5, arr[1] = 2. 5 > 2 is true, so arr[0] is greater than arr[1].
arr[0] = 3, arr[2] = 3. 3 == 3 is true, so elements are equal.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Access
ladders.desc: Access elements in an array using their indices.
ladders.explanation: Array indexing is the most fundamental concept in programming with arrays. An array is a contiguous block of memory storing elements, each accessible via a numerical index starting from 0. The intuition is that each position in the array has a unique address. To access an element: use syntax arr[index]. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection: All array operations build on this. Common mistakes: Using index out of bounds (e.g., arr[length] where valid indices are 0 to length-1), confusing 0-based with 1-based indexing. Real code pattern: value = arr[i].

examples.trace: Array has 4 elements at indices 0,1,2,3. Index 2 corresponds to third element, which is 30.
Index 0 is first element, which is 5.
